content.text: Hierarchical teams organize agents into functional groups — each team has a lead agent responsible for coordinating its workers and a higher-level coordinator that routes tasks between teams. The structure mirrors how human organizations decompose complex work: the executive layer makes strategic decisions, team leads manage execution within their domain, and workers perform focused, tool-enabled tasks. This three-tier arrangement gives you the routing flexibility of a supervisor at every layer while keeping each agent's scope and responsibility narrow.

attrs.alt: Three-tier hierarchy: executive coordinator routes to research/analysis/writing team leads, each of which coordinates its own workers

content.text: When to Use Hierarchical Teams
Tasks with clearly distinct phases
— research, then analysis, then writing each require different tools and expertise.
Independent teams
— teams can work in parallel without shared state conflicts.
Varying worker capabilities
— workers inside each team are specialists; the team lead knows which worker fits which sub-task.
Scale
— flat supervisor graphs become unwieldy past ~8 workers; teams group them logically.
Building Team Subgraphs in LangGraph

Each team is a compiled LangGraph subgraph. The team lead is a supervisor node inside the subgraph; workers are worker nodes. The subgraph has its own state type with only the fields that team needs — not the full pipeline state.
Wiring the Executive Coordinator

The executive coordinator is itself a supervisor that calls team subgraphs as nodes. It routes between teams and synthesizes the final result when all teams are done. Each team subgraph is invoked with a scoped input derived from the coordinator's state.
State Boundaries Between Layers

Each layer uses a distinct state type. The coordinator state holds high-level flags and aggregated outputs; team states hold only the data needed for that team's work. This boundary prevents a worker-level field from accidentally appearing in the executive layer's routing logic.
Layer
State fields
Model
Executive (L1)
task, team_done flags, aggregated outputs, final
claude-sonnet-5
Team Lead (L2)
team input, worker outputs, team summary
claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
Worker (L3)
sub-task input, tool output, errors
claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
Testing Hierarchical Teams
Test each layer independently by mocking the layer below it. This isolates failures and avoids expensive LLM calls in unit tests.

from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock

# --- Unit: team lead routing logic ---
def test_research_lead_routes_to_web_searcher_first():
    state = {
        "topic": "quantum computing",
        "web_results": "", "doc_contents": "", "summary": "",
        "errors": [], "next": "",
    }
    out = research_lead_node(state)
    assert out["next"] == "web_searcher"

def test_research_lead_finishes_when_all_workers_done():
    state = {
        "topic": "quantum computing",
        "web_results": "found", "doc_contents": "found", "summary": "",
        "errors": [], "next": "",
    }
    out = research_lead_node(state)
    assert out["next"] == "FINISH"

# --- Integration: team subgraph completes ---
def test_research_team_returns_output():
    mock_resp = MagicMock()
    mock_resp.content = "test output"
    with patch("my_graph.haiku") as mock_llm:
        mock_llm.invoke.return_value = mock_resp
        graph = build_research_team()
        result = graph.invoke({
            "topic": "AI safety", "web_results": "", "doc_contents": "",
            "summary": "", "errors": [], "next": "",
        })
    assert result["web_results"] == "test output"
    assert result["doc_contents"] == "test output"

# --- Integration: coordinator delegates to team ---
def test_coordinator_marks_research_done_after_team():
    mock_team = MagicMock()
    mock_team.invoke.return_value = {
        "web_results": "results", "doc_contents": "docs", "errors": [],
    }
    with patch("my_graph.research_team", mock_team):
        state = {
            "task": "explain LLMs",
            "research_done": False, "analysis_done": False,
            "research_output": "", "analysis_output": "", "final": "",
            "errors": [], "next": "",
        }
        out = call_research_team(state)
    assert out["research_done"] is True
    assert "results" in out["research_output"]
Parallel Team Execution

When teams are independent (research and analysis can run simultaneously before writing), the executive coordinator can fan them out in parallel using the Send API. This cuts total latency to the duration of the slowest team rather than the sum.
Error Isolation Across Teams

Because each team runs in an isolated subgraph, an error in the research team does not prevent the analysis team from completing. The executive coordinator collects team results with their error flags and routes to either full synthesis or a degraded synthesis that skips failed teams.
Summary
Hierarchical teams group agents into functional layers: executive coordinator (L1) routes between team leads (L2) who coordinate workers (L3).
Each team is a compiled LangGraph subgraph with its own state type — no global state leaks between layers.
Assign models by layer: Sonnet for executive routing, Haiku for team leads and workers.
Test each layer independently by mocking the layer below — isolates failures and keeps tests fast.
Use this pattern when tasks decompose into parallel functional domains and a flat supervisor would have too many workers to manage.
